coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-incorporated composite membrane towards enhanced organic solvent nanofiltration performance
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Zhijie
Wu, Xiaoli
Jiang, Yanjun
Li, Yifan
Huang, Jiajia
Hao, Lan
Zhang, Jie
Wang, Jingtao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a series of composite membranes hybridized by sub-5nm carbon dots (CDs) in polymer active layer were fabricated for organic solvent nanofiltration. The CDs with tailored functional groups were facilely synthesized and embedded into polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix, and then dip-coated on polyacrylonitrile support to prepare composite membranes through interfacial polymerization. The resultant membranes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, contact angle, thermal gravity analysis and tensile testing. The nanofiltration performances of membranes were evaluated by solvent uptake behavior, solvent flux and solute rejection, confirming that hybridization of small-sized CDs could well exert the advantages of PEI and CDs: the completely cross-linked PEI networks assured excellent solvent resistance and solute rejection ability, whilst CDs worked as selective nano-accelerators for solvent transfer via their functional groups. Carbonation degree acutely governed the acceleration ability of CDs for different solvents. And for instance, lowly carbonated CDs could efficiently adsorb polar solvents by the hydrophilic groups, thus providing a 54.3% permeance increase for isopropanol to 42.6Lm−2 h−1. Meanwhile, this kind of CDs also suppressed the transport of non-polar solvents and thereby enhanced membrane selectivity. In contrast, obvious increase of non-polar solvent uptaking and permeation was achieved by high-carbonation-degree CDs via their hydrophobic domains.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable carbonation degree
Composite membrane
Organic solvent nanofiltration
Selective accelerator

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs with different carbonation degrees
Characterization of CDs and composite membranes
Measurements of solvent uptake and swelling
Morphologies and physicochemical properties of composite membranes
Contact angle, solvent uptake, and area swelling of composite membranes
Operational stability of the composite membranes

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21576244 , 21506232 , and 51503184 ), Outstanding Young Talent Research Fund of Zhengzhou University ( 1521324001 ), and the Foundation of Henan Educational Committee ( 15A530001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs1 (inset: high-resolution TEM image), (b) TEM image of CDs3 (inset: high-resolution TEM image), (c) AFM image of CDs1 (inset: height profile along the white line on the image), (d) EDS elemental mappings of C and O on CDs1 and CDs4.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) water contact angles, (c) TGA curves, and (d) XRD patterns of the CDs.

SEM images of the surface of (a) PAN/PEI, (b) PAN/PEI-CDs2-10, (c) PAN/PEI-CDs2-30; AFM images of the surface of (d) PAN/PEI and (e) PAN/PEI-CDs2-10; the cross-sectional SEM images of (f and g) PAN/PEI-CDs2-10.
Stress-strain curves of (a) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10 and (b) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-X; TGA curves of (c) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10 and (d) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-X.
Water contact angles of (a) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10 and (b) PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-X.
(a) Solvent uptakes of PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10, (b) solvent uptakes, (c) area swellings, and (d) thickness swellings of free-standing PEI, PEI-CDs1-10, and PEI-CDs4-10.

The permeate fluxes of membranes for isopropanol, ethyl acetate, acetone, toluene and n-heptane under the trans-membrane pressure of (a and c) 4bar and (b and d) 10bar at room temperature; (e and f) flux enhancement ratios of membranes for isopropanol and n-heptane under 10bar.

(a) The rejections of PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10, (b) the rejections of PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-X, (c) the long-term operation performances of PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 in n-heptane solution using PEG 1000 as solute under 10bar, (d) the long-term operation performances of PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 in isopropanol solution using PEG 1000 as solute under 10bar.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-incorporated composite membrane towards enhanced organic solvent nanofiltration performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the preparation of the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a series of composite membranes hybridized by sub-5nm carbon dots (CDs) in polymer active layer were fabricated for organic solvent nanofiltration. The CDs with tailored functional groups were facilely synthesized and embedded into polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix, and then dip-coated on polyacrylonitrile support to prepare composite membranes through interfacial polymerization. The resultant membranes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, contact angle, thermal gravity analysis and tensile testing. The nanofiltration performances of membranes were evaluated by solvent uptake behavior, solvent flux and solute rejection, confirming that hybridization of small-sized CDs could well exert the advantages of PEI and CDs: the completely cross-linked PEI networks assured excellent solvent resistance and solute rejection ability, whilst CDs worked as selective nano-accelerators for solvent transfer via their functional groups. Carbonation degree acutely governed the acceleration ability of CDs for different solvents. And for instance, lowly carbonated CDs could efficiently adsorb polar solvents by the hydrophilic groups, thus providing a 54.3% permeance increase for isopropanol to 42.6Lm−2 h−1. Meanwhile, this kind of CDs also suppressed the transport of non-polar solvents and thereby enhanced membrane selectivity. In contrast, obvious increase of non-polar solvent uptaking and permeation was achieved by high-carbonation-degree CDs via their hydrophobic domains.

ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional (0D) materials, with many exceptional properties such as large specific surface area [1] and novel size effect [2], have garnered intense interests for many promising applications, especially in photoelectricity [3] and bio-medicine [4]. Among 0D materials, carbon dots (CDs) have triggered increasing attention since its first report in 2004 by Scrivens et al. [5]. Superior to many similar-size materials [6,7], the non-porous CDs were synthesized through an environmental-friendly and energy-saving approach ‒ one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine [8–14]. In such a way, monodisperse sub-5nm CDs with ultrahigh surface-to-volume ratio and different loadings of groups (–OH, –NH2 and –CO2H) were obtained, rendering them great potential in the fabrication of high-performance nanocomposites [15]. For example, despite being rarely reported, CDs might be distinct reinforcers for separation membranes by providing uniform-dispersed, selective transport sites, while large-sized fillers fall to do so.

On the other hand, in industrial process of pharmaceuticals, petrochemicals and foods, organic solvents (e.g., alcohols, esters, ketones, alkanes, aromatics) are the indispensable chemicals [16], the usage of which is just lower than that of water. Different from the natural resource of water, organic solvents are produced through energy-intensive and harsh-environment processes. Therefore, the separation and recycling of organic solvents are crucially important issues for saving resources and avoiding pollution [17,18]. Compared with the traditional flash chromatography [19], distillation [20] and extraction [21], organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) which employs a solvent-permeable and solute-blocking membrane might be one promising candidate as it can save energy and chemical to a certain extent [22]. For OSN, the properties of nanofiltration membrane are of vital significance, and high flux is highly needed to deal with a large volume of solvent solution. The low-cost, prominent processibility, and rejection ability make polymeric OSN membrane state-of-the-art at present [23]. However, polymeric membranes usually suffer from trade-off effect between permeation and rejection because of the wide distribution of free volume cavities of polymer chains. To overcome this inherent shortcoming, cross-linking and hybridization techniques have been utilized to adjust polymer chain free volume via covalent bonds or weak interactions, thus enhancing the sieving transfer ability [24]. By comparison, hybridizing nanofillers into polymer matrix is more attractive and universal since various materials can be employed for tunable free-volume regulation, and can provide additional transport carriers for solvent, which are difficult to be realized by cross-linking method [25].

For polymer-filler hybrid membrane, the nanostructure and component of fillers are critical in performance enhancement of OSN [26]. To sum up, nano-size to micro-size particles (MOFs [27], silica [28], zeolite [29]) and sheets (graphene oxide [30], MXene [25]) have been incorporated into the active layer of OSN membrane. The presence of nanochannels within the filler or facilitated transport sites on filler surface can accelerate solvent migration. For instance, Wu and co-workers created alcohol-selective pathways along MXene surface using the –OH groups, facilitating the isopropanol flux by 30.2% [25]. Together with the physical rejection of sheets on solutes, the resultant hybrid membrane readily overcame the trade-off effect in OSN membrane. However, the fabrication of desirable hybrid membrane that can well retain and combine the advantages of polymer and filler is still a challenge due to the following facts. The large-sized fillers (most > 100nm, especially nanosheets) would segment polymer bulk, sacrificing its inherent transfer ability and also making the membrane brittle [31]. Secondly, thick membrane (most > 1µm) is required to avoid defects due to the possible aggregation of fillers; however, thick membrane results in high membrane resistance and limited solvent flux. Considering size effect, small fillers at polymer-chain size scale (like CDs) might be more attractive because of their good compatibility and dispersion as well as ultrahigh surface-to-volume ratio [32]. Up to now, there is no report on CDs-based nanofiltration membranes, except for the paper by Li et al. for anticounterfeiting [33].

Herein, we reported a kind of polymer-CDs hybrid membrane and its application in organic solvent nanofiltration. Sub-5nm CDs with tunable carbonation degree, i.e. surface groups, were synthesized facilely as nanofillers. And polyethyleneimine (PEI) was selected as polymer matrix due to its excellent solvent resistance and film-forming ability. Cross-linked PEI-CDs layer was coated on hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile (PAN) support to obtain thin and defect-free composite membrane. The physicochemical properties and microstructures of membranes were investigated systematically. The OSN performances in terms of solvent resistance, solvent flux and solute rejection were evaluated in detail.

PEI (Mw 20,000Da) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China) without any post-treatment. TMC was supplied by Jianglai biochemical reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). PAN support with the molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 50,000Da was supplied by Zhongkeruiyang Membrane Engineering & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China), which was hydrolyzed by sodium hydroxide before utilization. Diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (99.5wt% purity, without water) were obtained from Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Organic solvents used to prepare membranes and for filtration experiments (n-hexane, glycerol, n-heptane, acetone, toluene isopropanol, and ethyl acetate) were obtained from Tianjin Kewei Chemistry Co., Ltd. Polyethylene glycol oligomers (PEG, Mws 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500 and 2000Da) were supplied by Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs with different carbonation degrees
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with different carbonation degrees were prepared by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis according to the literature [13], which was tuned by changing the composition of reaction solvents and thus the reaction temperature. Typically, citric acid (0.96g) was dissolved in the mixture of ultra-purified water (0.5g) and glycerol (1.5g). After citric acid being dissolved completely, DETA (540µL) was loaded in the above solution. The mixture was treated by ultrasonic for 5min at room temperature followed by heating in a 750W microwave oven for another 5min. During the reaction process, the color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow and then to black. Subsequently, the products were purified by washing with ethanol for three times to remove residual small molecules, and then dispersed in water. After centrifugation, CDs1 with the highest carbonation degree were yielded from the aqueous solution through freeze drying for 48h. CDs with lower carbonation degree were prepared by the same method with the amount of ultra-purified water increasing to 1.0g, 1.5g, and 2.0g, respectively, meanwhile the corresponding amount of glycerol decreased to 1.0g, 0.5g, and 0g, respectively. The resultant CDs were named as CDsM, where M (M = 1, 2, 3, and 4) represented the successively decreased carbonation degree.

The support (PAN) was tailored into disc with a diameter of 9.0cm and then immersed in NaOH solution (1M) for 1h at 50°C. The residual NaOH was removed by washing with water until the pH of the rinsed water reached about 7.0. The hydrolyzed PAN support was stored in water for its subsequent use.

A certain amount of CDs were dispersed into water (26.0g) by stirring for 1h at room temperature. PEI (4.0g, 50wt%) aqueous solution (24.0g) was prepared and stirred for 1h. Afterwards, the above solutions were mixed and stirred for a homogenous PEI-CDs solution (50.0g). Then, the mixture was cast onto the surface of hydrolyzed PAN support and kept for 10min. After removing the excess solution, the solution of TMC (0.5g) and n-hexane (50.0g) was cast onto PEI-CDs layer and kept for 2min to perform the cross-linking reaction. Then, the membrane was dried firstly in the air for 20min, and then at 60°C for 2h in the oven to evaporate residual n-hexane as well as complete cross-linking reaction. The filler content of 10wt% CDsM was utilized to ensure a close thickness of active layer (Fig. S1 and Table S1), and the resultant composite membranes were named as PAN/PEI-CDsM-10. Three other membranes prepared by embedding CDs2 were collectively named as PAN/PEI-CDs2-X, where X (X = 2, 5, and 30) represented the filler content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the membrane samples was recorded on a zinc selenium/diamond plate of a Nicolet (MAGNA-IR560) instrument with a resolution of 4cm−1 in the range of 4000–670cm−1 at room temperature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of CDs was performed on a FEI model TECNAI G2-TEM operated at 200kV. The samples were prepared by immersing 400-mesh carbon-coated copper grids into the water solutions of CDs, followed by drying under vacuum. Thermal gravity analysis (TGA) was conducted by TGA-50 SHIMADZU from 30 to 800°C at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. The distribution of component within CDs and membranes was analyzed by the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM7500F) instrument after freeze-fractured in liquid nitrogen and followed by sputtering with gold under the vacuum degree of 5Pa. Also, the surface and cross-sectional images of membranes were observed on this SEM. The crystalline structures of CDs were performed by a wide X-ray diffractometry (WXRD) using a RigakuD/max2500v/Pc (CuK 40kV, 200mV) in the range of 5–60°. The mechanical properties of membrane (1.0cm × 4.0cm) were tested using an Instron Mechanical tester (Testometric 350 AX) at an elongation rate of 2mmmin−1 under room temperature. The surface morphology and roughness of membranes were obtained by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Veeco MultiMode, United States). The static contact angles of CDs and membranes for water were investigated at room temperature on a contact angle goniometer (JC2000C Contact Angle Meter, Powereach Co.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of solvent uptake and swelling
ce:section.ce:para:
The membrane was tailored into a squared shaped sample (about 1.5cm × 1.5cm) after being dried in an oven at 60°C for 48h and then its weight (W
dry , g), area (A
dry , cm2), and thickness (l
dry , cm) were measured accurately. Afterwards, the membrane sample was immersed into a certain pure organic solvent at room temperature for 72h. The swollen sample was taken out and then re-measured (W
wet , g, A
wet , cm2, and l
wet , cm) immediately when the excess solvent was wiped away using a tissue. The solvent uptake and swelling were defined below:
(1)
Solvent
uptake
( % )
=
(
W
w e t
−
W
d r y
)
/
W
d r y
× 100
(2)
Area
swelling
( % )
=
(
A
w e t
−
A
d r y
)
/
A
d r y
× 100
(3)
Thickness
swelling
( % )
=
(
l
w e t
−
l
d r y
)
/
l
d r y
× 100
The values of solvent uptake and swelling degree of the membrane were determined by the average of three measurements.

Nanofiltration performances were conducted in a homemade dead-end cell with a teflon-coated magnetic stirring bar (300rpm) to minimize concentration polarization, and the volume of cell was appropriate 200cm3. All the experiments were carried out in a N2 pressurized cell and the testing pressures were 4 and 10bar with the effective membrane area of 19.6cm2. Prior to the testing, the membrane was immersed in the experiment solvents, including n-heptane, toluene, acetone, ethyl acetate, and isopropanol, for 72h to get a complete equilibration. Then the sample was immobilized by an O-ring. The sample was pre-compacted with solvent at 2bar for about 30min, in order to obtain a steady permeation state. Subsequently, the pressure was turned to 4 or 10bar. In order to investigate the selectivity of composite membrane, a mixed solution composed of isopropanol and n-heptane with a mole ratio of 1:1 was utilized as feed solution. The permeate solution was analyzed by gas chromatography device, and then the selectivity of isopropanol/n-heptane was calculated. To calculate the flux, the permeate liquid was collected and weighted in a tube, which was surrounded by liquid nitrogen to reduce solvent volatilization. Solvent flux (F, Lm−2 h−1) was defined as the permeate volume (V, L) per unit area (A, m2) per unit time (t, h) using the following equation:
(4)
F = V /
(
A t
)

For the rejection test, a series of PEG with the molecular weights of 200–2000Da were dissolved in isopropanol as feed solution (500mgL−1). Quantitative analysis of PEG in permeate solution was acquired by Shaffer method. The permeate liquid was colored by BaCl2/I2 and then scanned on UV–vis (Shimadzu 2450) to draw a standard curve (PEG concentration vs. absorbance). Thereafter, the PEG concentration in permeate solution would be calculated by its absorbance according to the standard curve. The membrane samples were pre-compacted at 4bar for 30min firstly, followed by turning the pressure to 10bar and taking the permeate solution for another 30min. The speed of stirrer was controlled at 300rpm to minimize concentration polarization. The rejection (R, %) was calculated from the following equation:
(5)
R =
(
1 –
C
p
/
C
f
)
× 100
Where C
p (mgL−1) and C
f (mgL−1) were the concentrations of PEG in permeate and feed solutions, respectively.

The CDs were synthesized by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis through inter- and intra-molecular dehydration between citric acid and diethylenetriamine followed by a further carbonization. The carbonation degree, videlicet, the functional groups on CDs, varied delicately with reaction temperature, which was tailored by glycerol concentration in reaction solution. Four carbonation degrees were obtained by decreasing glycerol mass from 1.5g to 1.0g, 0.5g, 0g, respectively, corresponding to CDs1, CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4. It should be noted that CDs4 was fabricated using water as solvent, which was actually a carbonization-free condensation. Here, purified CDs were re-dispersed in water and then freeze dried to acquire monodisperse CDs. The topographic morphology of CDs, take CDs1 as an example, was measured by AFM, which revealed a typical 0D structure with 2–4nm height for single CDs1 ( Fig. 1c). Further, clear morphologies of CDs were probed by TEM as shown in Fig. 1a (CDs1, the highest carbonation degree) and 1b (CDs3, the low carbonation degree). The CDs presented a uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation regardless of carbonation degree, and the size of which ranged from 2 to 5nm. This situation was similar to that in literature [34]. The high reaction temperature allowed CDs1 forming highly carbonated lattice structure (inset in Fig. 1a) because of the reduction of hydrophilic –NH2 and –CO2H [8]. In contrast, the abundant hydrophilic groups in CDs3 generated complex inter-CD interaction, making them difficult to form ordered stacking and hence no lattice (inset in Fig. 1b) [35]. For CDs4, it was difficult to gain its TEM and AFM images due to the dis-order stacking and aggregation.

The chemical components of CDs were investigated by EDS and FTIR as depicted in Figs. 1d and 2a, respectively. CDs1 exhibited high C element loading but low value for O element, and differently, CDs4 attained higher O element loading than CDs1. The C and O distribution in EDS mappings verified that the CDs1 possess much higher carbonation degree which means few oxygen-contained groups. In addition, all the CDs displayed the characteristic bands for C˭O, N–H, and O–H at 1720, 1543, and 1390cm−1 (dashed lines), respectively, in agreement with the result in literatures [36,37]. Furthermore, a gradual increase of band intensity could be observed as the carbonation degree decreased from CDs1 to CDs4, indicating and increase of group loading amount. The reduction of hydrophilic groups also resulted in the alteration of contact angles from 29.5° to 42.1° for the CDs from CDs4 to CDs1 as illustrated in Fig. 2b [38]. The thermal property of CDs in Fig. 2c suggested that all CDs were thermally stable up to 200°C and met the requirement of practical application. As for the first stage of moisture evaporation (25–250°C), high hydrophilic group loading gave CDs4 strong water adsorption ability and a consequent highest weight-loss (15.4%), whereas CDs1 attained the lowest one of 7.5%. Then the deoxygenation of oxygen-containing groups and partial backbone generated an obvious weight reduction from 250 to 460°C. At 800°C, all CDs left a close char yield of about 29.6% after the complete decomposition of C–C bonds. The XRD patterns displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20° (Fig. 2d), which suggested that all CDs were amorphous materials. The diffraction peak of CDs1 was higher than other CDsM. Together with the situation of TEM, such phenomenon implied that CDs1 possessed a higher carbonation degree.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and physicochemical properties of composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were incorporated into PEI matrix to fabricate hybrid active layer on a hydrolyzed PAN support for the composite membrane: PAN/PEI-CDs2-X and PAN/PEI-CDsM-10. The morphologies of above membranes were examined by SEM and AFM as shown in Fig. 3. The insert in Fig. 3a revealed that the active layer displayed white color on PAN support, while the addition of 10wt% CDs2 (black) altered membrane color to light yellow (the insert in Fig. 3b). Clearer morphology by SEM suggested that the surface of PAN/PEI (Fig. 3a) was homogeneous and smooth without obvious voids or defects. By comparison, PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 (Fig. 3b) presented a relatively rough surface due to the presence of CDs in PEI bulk. When the loading increased up to 30wt%, in spite of aggregations, the good compatibility between sub-5nm CDs and PEI chains enabled a complete active layer for PAN/PEI-CDs2-30 (Fig. 3c) without obvious defects. Further, AFM was used to estimate the surface morphology of membranes as shown in Fig. 3d and e, which showed a similar ridge-and-valley structure between PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 [39]. And the incorporation of CDs2 made the active layer rougher with an increase of surface roughness (Ra) from 69.5nm to 83.5nm. This was greatly difficult for thin organic-inorganic layer with such high filler loading, because defects or even phase separation was inclined to appear for large-sized fillers (most > 100nm, especially nanosheets) when the loading was over 10% [25]. Cross-sectional image of PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 also revealed a uniform dispersion of CDs2 within PEI because of the good compatibility (Fig. 3f), and no visible CDs2 particle could be observed at a high magnification. The dense and uniform active layer was tightly adhered on PAN support with the thickness of around 200nm, comparable to other thin film.

Mechanical property of the membranes in the light of stress-strain curves was depicted in Fig. 4a and b. The curves verified that PAN/PEI membrane acquired acceptable mechanical stability with the tensile strength of 14.4MPa and Young's modulus of 200.6MPa, together with the elongation at break of 17.5%. By comparison, the incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the tensile strength and Young's modulus of composite membranes, whilst reducing their elongation at break. For instance, 10wt% CDs4 resulted in a 36.1% increase of tensile strength (to 19.6MPa) and a 64.6% increase of Young's modulus (to 330.2MPa). This should be also ascribed to the interfacial interactions and hence the inhibition of PEI chain motion. And as the carbonation degree decreased (from CDs1 to CDs4), the enhancement of interfacial interactions brought gradual increase of both tensile strength and Young's modulus. Similarly, increasing the CDs2 loading could generate more interactions with PEI matrix, thus offering higher mechanical stability (Fig. 4b). However, the aggregation of CDs2 under 30wt% loading made the mechanical behavior of PAN/PEI-CDs2-30 a little lower than that of PAN/PEI-CDs2-10. Therefore, 10wt% loading was chosen for preparing the other composite membranes with CDs1, CDs3 and CDs4.

FTIR spectra of the membranes in Fig. S2 gave rise to the stretching vibration bands of O–H, N–H, and C˭O in cross-linked PEI layer at 1390, 1543, and 1720cm−1, respectively [36,37]. TGA curves of PAN/PEI, PAN/PEI-CDs1-10, and PAN/PEI-CDs4-10 were depicted in Fig. 4c, which all revealed an analogous three-stage weight-loss. The first stage was the water evaporation from membranes (around 30–300°C), whilst the degradation of side-chains of PEI, PAN and CDs were regarded as the second stage (around 300–400°C). The last stage was from the pyrolysis of the backbones of polymer chains and CDs. Although CDs1 only had an onset temperature of 180°C, the hybridization effect helped PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 attain the higher onset temperature (240°C) than the 160°C of PAN/PEI. The interfacial interactions might impart this improvement by inhibiting PEI chain motion. Similar to the observation of CDs, PAN/PEI-CDs4-10 displayed higher weight-loss in the first stage than PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 as a result of the numerous hydrophilic groups on CDs4. With the increase of CDs2 loading from 10wt% to 30wt%, a slight enhancement of thermal stability occurred because of the increase of interfacial interactions (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle, solvent uptake, and area swelling of composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The affinity of membrane matrix to solvent is critical for membrane permeation and selectivity by dictating membrane dissolution ability. Contact angle of the membranes was performed and the results were shown in Fig. 5. Hydrophilic nature of PEI chains gave a low contact angle of 56.3° to PAN/PEI, close to the result in literature [25]. By comparison, the addition of CDs further enhanced membrane hydrophilic nature and reduced the contact angle to below 47.6°. This was reasonably attributed to lower contact angle of CDs coupled with the rougher membrane surface that promoted droplet spreading. Furthermore, the contact angles of composite membranes varied with CDs kind and followed the order of PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 (47.6°)>PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 (35.5°)>PAN/PEI-CDs3-10 (34.1°)>PAN/PEI-CDs4-10 (29.3°) as Fig. 5a. And the angle difference between membranes was huger than that of corresponding CDs, probably a result of the increase in membrane roughness. Fig. 5b showed that as the CDs2 loading increased from 2wt% to 30wt%, the influence of CDs on membrane hydrophilic nature became stronger as testified by the further reduction of contact angle.

Moderate solvent uptake and swelling were needed to enlarge the free volume of active layer for fast solvent permeation. Fig. 6a and Fig. S3a showed the solvent uptakes of membranes in five commonly used solvents, including polar isopropanol, ethyl acetate, acetone and non-polar toluene and n-heptane. The hydrophilic groups in PEI matrix endowed PAN/PEI with higher isopropanol, ethyl acetate, and acetone uptakes of 30.7%, 25.6%, 20.4%, respectively, while lower uptakes for toluene (17.0%) and n-heptane (16.2%) (Fig. 6a). Interestingly, the corresponding area swellings of PAN/PEI were much lower of below 5% as illustrated in Fig. S3b and S3c. Such results inferred that the part of absorbed solvent was stored in the micropores of PAN support, and this layer might also limit the swelling of active layer. To directly evaluate the property of active layer, free-standing active layers were synthesized (Scheme S1) and measured using these solvents and the results were shown in Fig. 6b–d. The solvent uptakes of free-standing PEI for isopropanol, ethyl acetate, acetone, toluene, and n-heptane were 138%, 113%, 88%, 72%, and 68%, respectively. Correspondingly, the area swellings were 45%, 42%, 39%, 34%, and 32%, respectively, while the thickness swellings were 101%, 93%, 87%, 66%, and 50%. Such results implied that cross-linked PEI matrix could adsorb solvents with reasonable swellings. Compared with the solvent uptakes of PEI, it was found that the uptake ability for polar solvent and non-polar solvent was inconsistent or even inverse after embedding CDs. Specifically, the presence of CDs4 with abundant hydrophilic groups efficiently assisted the active layer to interact and adsorb polar solvents whilst suppressing the adsorption for non-polar solvents. For example, PEI-CDs4-10 exhibited a 43% increase of isopropanol uptake and a 50% decrease of n-heptane uptake. Furthermore, this alteration of solvent uptake led to a roughly analogous change tendency of area and thickness swellings of active layer (Fig. 6c and d). This indicated that more polar solvent molecules were adsorbed and stayed in active layer. As the carbonation degree increased (from CDs4 to CDs1), the promotion effect for polar solvent uptake and inhibition effect for non-polar solvent uptake of CDs became weak as a result of the reduction of hydrophilic groups. Especially for PEI-CDs1-10, it oppositely attained a reduction of polar solvent uptake (by 7.2% for isopropanol) but an enhancement for non-polar solvent uptake (by 8.8% for n-heptane). Such finding implied that CDs1 with highly carbonated degree formed hydrophobic domains to sorb and store toluene and n-heptane molecules, and this was in accordance with its lattice structure. This can be further confirmed by the distinct swelling behaviors. PEI-CDs1-10 achieved an increased swelling degree in non-polar solvent but a reduced one in polar solvent. Specifically, its thickness swelling for n-heptane was increased to 66% whereas the value for isopropanol was reduced to 93%. In summary, these results demonstrated that the CDs could act as distinct organic solvent bonding sites to adsorb and diffuse solvent in membrane, and the functional groups on CDs provided a subtle regulation of membrane affinity for different solvents. Besides, the robust PAN support could efficiently inhibit active layer swelling, thus permitting an excellent solvent-resistance to the composite membrane.

The OSN performances of membranes were evaluated by solvent flux, solute rejection, and operation stability according to the method in literature [40]. The above-mentioned five solvents were utilized as indicators to monitor membrane performances at the trans-membrane pressures of 4 and 10bar. In general, elevating transfer impetus by increasing trans-membrane pressure was a powerful manner to enhance membrane transport property. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluxes of isopropanol, ethyl acetate, acetone, toluene, and n-heptane for PAN/PEI were 19.2, 16.1, 8.0, 5.9, and 4.2Lm−2 h−1 under 4bar, respectively, which elevated to 27.6, 19.5, 14.7, 8.8, and 6.6Lm−2 h−1 when pressure increased to 10bar [41]. Upon CDs incorporation, albeit having slight differences, all composite membranes displayed the same flux order for these five solvents. The much higher flux of isopropanol, ethyl acetate and acetone should again result from the hydrophilic nature of PEI matrix. Besides, the transfer rate order (i.e., n-heptane<toluene<acetone<ethyl acetate<isopropanol) was roughly consistent with the order of solubility parameter (corresponding to polarity) rather than solvent viscosity as listed in Table S2. As for acetone and ethyl acetate, it was clear that the solubility parameters were similar. Adjoining with hydrogen bonding site, the -CH2CH3 of ethyl acetate was much larger than -CH3 of acetone, leading to stronger swelling due to the absence of self-hydrogen bond cluster of solvent—perhaps this explained the difference of the permeation of these two solvents. Such observations indicated that solution-diffusion model might be the dominant mechanism in the as-prepared membranes, in accordance with the observations of other PEI-based membranes [25,38]. For the CDs-filled membranes, it was found that the hydrophilic/hydrophobic nature of CDs could govern membrane solvent permeation, especially for the strong polar and non-polar solvents. For instance, Fig. 7a, b, and e clearly revealed that the addition of CDs4 significantly promoted the transport of isopropanol and ethyl acetate but suppressed the migration of toluene and n-heptane, and 10wt% CDs4 resulted in a 54.3% increase of isopropanol flux to 42.6Lm−2 h−1 and meanwhile a 29.8% decrease of n-heptane flux to 4.6Lm−2 h−1. This finding also suggested that the CDs4 could readily enhance the polar solvent flux of OSN membrane. Interestingly, the CDs1 displayed opposite functions on solvent transport, and PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 showed enhanced non-polar solvent permeation but slowed permeation of polar solvents. As depicted in Fig. 7e, the flux of n-heptane increased to 8.6Lm−2 h−1 by 31.2% along with a 4.6% reduction for isopropanol flux when filling 10wt% CDs1, indicating the facilitated permeation for non-polar solvent. Considering the obvious alteration of solvent uptake and membrane swelling, these results verified the efficient acceleration of CDs for solvent molecules by facilitating their dissolution and providing transfer sites using the high-loading, uniform functional groups. Taking CDs4 as an example, the –OH, –NH2 and –CO2H groups helped CDs4 preferentially adsorb polar molecules through hydrogen-bonding interaction, and then assisted them continuous migration as hopping sites [42]. Therefore, as the loading of functional groups reduced, the facilitated transport for polar solvents of CDs on membrane became weakened. For instance, the improvement degrees of isopropanol flux were 54.3%, 40.2%, and 37.4% for the composite membranes with CDs4, CDs3 and CDs2, respectively. The influence of CDs loading on solvent permeation was illustrated in Fig. 7c and d using CDs2 as an example. The results suggested that elevating CDs loading in membrane would produce stronger influence on solvent transport by providing more selective accelerators. With the CDs2 content increasing from 2wt% to 5wt% and 10wt%, the isopropanol flux elevated to from 30.9 to 34.7 and 37.9Lm−2 h−1 with the increase of 12.0%, 25.7%, 37.3% when compared to the control membrane, respectively. While the corresponding n-heptane flux dropped from 6.2 to 5.8, and 5.4Lm−2 h−1 with the reduction of 6.1%, 12.1% and 18.2%. The liner alteration of both two kinds of solvent flux with CDs loading indicated that CDs had obvious regulation ability of active layer for solvent transfer. This could also be proved by the performances of PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 that displayed lower contact angle (47.6° vs. 56.3° of PAN/PEI) but achieved 30.3% increase of n-heptane flux, implying the efficient transport ability of these sub-5nm CDs. The slight reduction of solvent flux of PAN/PEI-CDs2-30 was probably due to the increased thickness of active layer (Fig. S1 and Table S1). In addition, the aggregation of CDs2 might be another factor, in agreement with the report of other organic-inorganic composite membranes [43,44].

Furthermore, in order to investigate the selectivity of CDs towards different solvents, we measured the flux and selectivity of representative membranes using a mixed solution. The solution was composed of isopropanol and n-heptane due to the biggest difference of polarity and good intersolubility. As depicted in Table S3, the fluxes of isopropanol and n-heptane for PAN/PEI were 27.8 and 4.8Lm−2 h−1 under 10bar, respectively, meaning an isopropanol/n-heptane selectivity of 5.8. Such finding derived from the hydrophilic nature of PEI matrix. By comparison, incorporating 10wt% CDs4 not only elevated isopropanol flux to 44.1Lm−2 h−1 but also increased the selectivity to 9.5. This directly confirmed the selective transfer ability of CDs4 for polar solvent because of the abundant hydrophilic groups. In contrast, CDs1 showed an opposite function and offered a decreased selectivity of 3.2.

Rejection property was another key parameter for OSN membrane, and high rejection was needed for adequate operation efficiency. Herein, the rejection measurement was conducted using PEG (Mws 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500, and 2000) as solute and isopropanol as solvent due to its highest flux. The results were depicted in Fig. 8a and b. As reported previously, PEI-based membrane displayed acceptable rejection ability after fully cross-linking that created small-sized networks. The obtained rejections showed that the rejection ability of all membranes was improved with the increase of PEG Mw. Moreover, the molecular weight cutoff was below 800. When compared with the behavior of PAN/PEI, the composite membrane displayed roughly identical rejection ability to solute molecules. This finding was quite different from the obvious increase of membrane rejection by hybridizing large-sized fillers, which were demonstrated to physically block the transfer of solute. In contrast, the composite membrane displayed a slight reduction of rejection. Such results indicated that sub-5nm CDs might slightly alter the integrated structure of PEI bulk, and the in apparent rejection reduction presumably resulted from the CDs, which facilitated the migration of oxygen-containing PEG. Accordingly, PAN/PEI-CDs4-10 had the lowest rejection values because of the abundant hydrophilic groups on CDs4 surface. While for CDs with low group loading, the resultant composite membranes showed almost constant rejection with the increase of CDs2 content especially for PEG Mw above 1000 (Fig. 8b). The aggregation of CDs under 30wt% loading produced physical inhibition on PEG transport, and thus elevated membrane rejection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operational stability of the composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 (using n-heptane as solvent), and PAN/PEI and PAN/PEI-CDs2-10 (using isopropanol as solvent) were chosen as representatives for the evaluation of operation stability. The flux and rejection (using PEG 1000 as solute) were measured in a cross-flow unit for 24h under 10bar. As depicted in Fig. 8c, the n-heptane flux of PAN/PEI reduced from 6.7 to 5.2Lm−2 h−1 with a reduction of 22.4% during the beginning 6h due to membrane compaction and pore blockage [25]. By comparison, PAN/PEI-CDs1-10 showed a flux reduction of 20.2%, indicating that the incorporation of CDs1 weakened the reduction of flux. Similarly, the addition of CDs2 could decrease the flux reduction of membrane for isopropanol (19.2% vs. 21.3% for PAN/PEI) as illustrate in Fig. 8d. On the other hand, membrane compaction and pore blockage afforded a slight improvement of rejection to above 98% for PEG 1000 during the testing process. Despite the flux reduction, the as-prepared membranes showed potential operational stability for OSN application.

In this study, novel composite membranes based on polymer-CDs active layer were prepared for organic solvent nanofiltration. Sub-5nm 0D carbon dots were facilely synthesized with tailed functional groups, which were then incorporated into PEI matrix to prepare composite membranes. The results demonstrated that although being hybridized with high loading CDs, PEI matrix kept its inherent physicochemical properties without obvious interference from these nanoscale fillers. The cross-linked networks of PEI conferred excellent solvent resistance, solute rejection (molecular weight cutoff below 800) and adequate operation stability on composite membranes. While the uniformly-distributed CDs worked as efficient and selective nano-accelerators for solvent transport. Specifically, lowly carbonated CDs could facilitate polar solvent migration through membrane by providing bonding sites of hydrophilic groups (–OH, –CO2H, –NH2). For instance, the membrane embedded CDs4 displayed up to 54.3% increase of solvent flux and 40.5% increase of solvent uptake. Meanwhile, such CDs slightly suppressed the uptake and permeation for non-polar solvents, thus enhancing OSN membrane permeation for polar solvents. In contrast, highly carbonated CDs acted as non-polar solvent accelerators using their hydrophobic domains whilst blocking the transport of polar solvents. The unique hybridization strategy delineated herein may pave the way for well-designed nanohybrid or nanocomposite membranes that maximally coupled the advantages of both polymers and fillers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576244, 21506232, and 51503184), Outstanding Young Talent Research Fund of Zhengzhou University (1521324001), and the Foundation of Henan Educational Committee (15A530001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2017.11.051
